title: Your first class
instruction:
A class is a blueprint for creating objects with the same structure.

- `constructor` runs when you do `new ClassName()`
- `this` inside a class refers to the instance being created
- Methods are defined without the `function` keyword

Run this.
successMessage: Class → new User() creates an instance. constructor sets up the properties. Methods are shared by all instances (they live on the prototype — more on that in Chapter 14).

title: Add methods to a class
instruction:
Add two methods to the `BankAccount` class:
- `deposit(amount)` — add to balance
- `getBalance()` — return balance formatted as `'$0.00'`

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: $425.00 — correct! Methods access instance data via this. The balance is private to each account instance.

title: Getters and setters
instruction:
Getters and setters look like properties but call functions:

```js
get fullName() { return `${this.first} ${this.last}`; }
set fullName(name) { [this.first, this.last] = name.split(" "); }
```

Access: `user.fullName` (no `()` — it's not called like a method).

Add a getter `fullName` and setter `fullName` to the Person class.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Getters look like properties. Setters intercept assignment. React's controlled inputs, Vue's computed properties — all use this getter/setter pattern.

title: Static methods — no instance needed
instruction:
`static` methods belong to the class itself, not instances:

```js
User.create(...)  // static — no new needed
user.greet()      // instance — needs an instance
```

Useful for utility functions and factory methods.

Add a static `validate(email)` and `fromJSON(json)` factory.
hint:
static validate(email) { return email.includes('@'); }
static fromJSON(json) { return new User(json.name, json.email); }

codeChecks.message: Add: static fromJSON(json) { return new User(...) }
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: User.validate() and User.fromJSON() — called on the class, not an instance. Factory methods (fromJSON, fromString, create) are the most common use of static.

title: Private fields with #
successMessage: #balance is truly private — not a convention, a language feature. You cannot access it from outside the class, even in tests. JavaScript finally caught up to Java and Python.

title: Method chaining
instruction:
Method chaining lets you call multiple methods in sequence:

```js
queryBuilder.select('name').where('age > 18').limit(10).build()
```

Make methods chainable by returning `this`:

```js
where(condition) {
  this.conditions.push(condition);
  return this;  // enables chaining!
}
```

Add `select`, `where`, and `limit` to the QueryBuilder.
expectedOutput: SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE age > 18 AND active = true LIMIT 10

codeChecks.message: Add select method that returns this.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Method chaining — elegant and readable. jQuery, Mongoose, Knex, Prisma all use this pattern. The key: return this from every chainable method.

title: Build a shopping cart class
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `ShoppingCart` class:
- Private `#items` array
- `add(product, quantity)` — add/update item
- `remove(productId)` — remove item
- `get total()` — returns sum of all item prices × quantities
- `get count()` — total item count
- `clear()` — empty the cart
- `summary()` — returns formatted cart contents

Test with at least 4 operations.

codeChecks.message: Use array methods to calculate totals.

successMessage: A full shopping cart with private state, getters, and methods. This is the structure of real e-commerce cart implementations. Chapter 12 done!
